appeal_letter:
Re: Appeal of Denial — Transfer to Inpatient Rehabilitation Facility (IRF)

Dear UnitedHealthcare Appeals Reviewer,

I am writing to appeal the denial of the prior authorization request to transfer the patient from acute hospital care to an Inpatient Rehabilitation Facility (IRF). This letter asks that the decision be reconsidered based on the patient's current and accurately documented therapy tolerance.

The denial stated that, under UnitedHealthcare guidelines, IRF admission requires evidence that the patient can actively participate in and benefit from at least three hours of intensive therapy per day. The denial stated that the hospital physical therapist's notes indicated the patient fatigued after 30 minutes of therapy, and on that basis authorized a transfer to a Skilled Nursing Facility (SNF) instead.

I want to confront the exact criterion the denial relied on directly. The three-hour intensive-therapy threshold is the correct standard to apply, and the question is whether the documented facts, accurately and currently assessed, meet it. The denial rested on a single data point — a note of fatigue after 30 minutes of therapy. The clinical context indicates that this note reflected the day immediately following a severe stroke, which is the point at which the lowest therapy tolerance would be expected. Relying on that single early note does not accurately represent the patient's current ability to participate.

The individualized facts indicate that since that initial day the patient has been tolerating approximately two hours of therapy per day, and the clinical context describes the patient as highly motivated. A trajectory of rapidly increasing tolerance — from 30 minutes on the day after the stroke to roughly two hours since — speaks directly to the guideline's actual concern, which is whether the patient can participate in and benefit from intensive therapy. I ask the reviewer to evaluate the three-hour criterion against the patient's current and improving tolerance rather than against the single earliest note, and to consider that the relevant clinical question is the patient's capacity going forward.

I also note that the level-of-care consequence is significant. The clinical context indicates that an SNF will not provide the intensive daily therapy the patient needs to regain walking ability, which makes accurate application of the criterion particularly important here.

I further note a procedural point. The denial as provided does not state the deadline for filing this appeal, the process for submitting it, or the patient's right to a full and fair review. I respectfully assert the patient's right to a complete and fair review and request that the applicable appeal rights, deadlines, and procedures be provided in writing.

Requested action: I ask that UnitedHealthcare overturn the denial and approve the transfer to the Inpatient Rehabilitation Facility based on the patient's current documented therapy tolerance and trajectory; or alternatively reassess the request using up-to-date therapy notes rather than the single note from the day immediately following the stroke, and provide a written explanation of how the three-hour criterion was applied.

The hospital care team and treating physical therapist can provide updated therapy documentation on request.

Not legal or medical advice. Draft assistance only.

Respectfully,
On behalf of the Patient
missing_evidence_checklist: Updated physical therapy notes documenting the patient's current daily therapy tolerance (approximately two hours per day)
Dated therapy logs showing the trajectory from the day after the stroke to the present
Treating physician's or physiatrist's statement on the patient's ability to participate in and benefit from intensive therapy
Documentation clarifying that the cited 30-minute fatigue note was from the day immediately following the stroke
The specific UnitedHealthcare IRF admission guideline criteria cited in the denial
The applicable appeal deadline, filing procedure, and statement of the patient's review rights
